title: The reactivity series — why some metals are more reactive than others
examTip: AQA Paper 1, Topic 4 (Chemical Changes). Expect questions on predicting displacement reactions, explaining extraction methods, and writing word or symbol equations for metal reactions with water and acid.
introTips: Covers the order of the reactivity series and what it means
You'll be able to predict displacement reactions and write equations
By the end you can explain why we extract different metals in different ways
intro: Every time you see an iron bridge slowly rusting while a gold ring stays shiny for centuries, you're seeing the reactivity series in action. The reactivity series is one of the most useful tools in chemistry — it lets you predict whether a reaction will happen, what the products will be, and how a metal was extracted from the ground. AQA tests it on Paper 1, and it rewards students who can apply the series rather than just recite it.

keyPoints.heading: The reactivity series — order and meaning
keyPoints.content: The reactivity series is a list of metals arranged in order of their reactivity, from most reactive at the top to least reactive at the bottom. The order is: Potassium (K), Sodium (Na), Lithium (Li), Calcium (Ca), Magnesium (Mg), Aluminium (Al), Zinc (Zn), Iron (Fe), Nickel (Ni), Tin (Sn), Lead (Pb), Copper (Cu), Silver (Ag), Gold (Au), Platinum (Pt). A useful mnemonic is: Please Stop Letting Crazy Monkeys Annoy Zesty Intelligent Nice Smart Lions, Causing Serious Grief Periodically. Reactivity relates to how easily a metal loses electrons to form positive ions — more reactive metals lose electrons more readily.
writeBullets: Reactivity series — metals in order from most reactive (K) to least reactive (Pt)
Order — K, Na, Li, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Ni, Sn, Pb, Cu, Ag, Au, Pt
Reactivity links to ease of losing electrons — more reactive = loses electrons more easily
More reactive metals — corrode quickly, react vigorously with water/acid
Less reactive metals — resist corrosion, found as native metals (e.g. gold, platinum)

keyTerms.def: A list of metals ranked from most to least reactive based on how readily they lose electrons
A metal found uncombined in nature because it is too unreactive to form compounds (e.g. gold, platinum)

keyPoints.memTip: First 6 in order: K Na Li Ca Mg Al — try: 'King Napoleon Likes Chocolate Milk Always'. Cover your notes and write all 15 metals in order from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: You don't need to memorise every metal, but you do need to know the top 8 (K to Fe) confidently, and be able to use a given series in context questions. AQA often provides a partial list and asks you to apply it.

keyPoints.heading: Reactions of metals with water and acid
keyPoints.content: The position of a metal in the reactivity series predicts how vigorously it reacts with water and with dilute acid. Potassium, sodium, and lithium react vigorously (even violently) with cold water, producing a metal hydroxide and hydrogen gas. For example: sodium + water → sodium hydroxide + hydrogen. Calcium reacts steadily with cold water (fizzes). Magnesium reacts slowly with cold water but vigorously with dilute acid. Zinc and iron react with dilute acid but not (noticeably) with cold water. Copper, silver, gold do not react with dilute acid or water. When metals react with acids, the products are a salt and hydrogen. The hydrogen can be tested with a lighted splint — it gives a squeaky pop.
writeBullets: K, Na, Li — react violently with cold water → metal hydroxide + hydrogen
Ca — reacts steadily with cold water → calcium hydroxide + hydrogen
Mg — slow with cold water; vigorous with dilute acid
Zn, Fe — react with dilute acid → salt + hydrogen; no visible reaction with cold water
Cu, Ag, Au — no reaction with water or dilute acid
Hydrogen test — lighted splint gives a squeaky pop

keyTerms.def: The test for hydrogen gas: a lighted splint held near the gas produces a squeaky pop sound
The product formed when a reactive metal reacts with water (e.g. sodium hydroxide, NaOH)

keyPoints.memTip: Vigorous → Steady → Slow → Nothing: K/Na/Li react explosively, Ca fizzes, Mg is slow, Cu does nothing. Cover and write the word equation for sodium reacting with water from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: When writing equations for metal + acid, the salt name comes from the metal and the acid: hydrochloric acid gives chlorides, sulfuric acid gives sulfates, nitric acid gives nitrates. Always include hydrogen as a product.

keyPoints.heading: Displacement reactions
keyPoints.content: A displacement reaction occurs when a more reactive metal pushes a less reactive metal out of its compound. The more reactive metal loses electrons (is oxidised) and the metal ion in solution gains electrons (is reduced). For example: iron + copper sulfate solution → iron sulfate + copper. Iron is higher in the reactivity series than copper, so iron displaces copper. You can observe this: the iron turns orange/red as copper metal coats it, and the blue solution fades. If you try to add copper to iron sulfate solution, nothing happens — copper is less reactive than iron and cannot displace it. OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons).
writeBullets: Displacement — more reactive metal pushes less reactive metal out of its compound
Example — Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu (iron displaces copper)
Oxidation — the more reactive metal loses electrons (OIL)
Reduction — the metal ion in solution gains electrons (RIG)
Observation — solution changes colour; solid metal coats the reactive metal
No reaction — if the added metal is less reactive than the metal in solution

keyTerms.def: A reaction where a more reactive metal replaces a less reactive metal from its compound in solution
Loss of electrons (OIL — Oxidation Is Loss)
Gain of electrons (RIG — Reduction Is Gain)
Memory aid: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)

keyPoints.memTip: OIL RIG — write it large on your page and remember: the more reactive metal is always oxidised (loses electrons). Cover and write the ionic equation for iron displacing copper from memory: Fe + Cu²⁺ → Fe²⁺ + Cu.
keyPoints.tutor_note: AQA often asks you to write ionic equations for displacement reactions. Strip out the spectator ions (the ones that don't change) and only show what actually reacts. Fe + Cu²⁺ → Fe²⁺ + Cu is the ionic equation for iron + copper sulfate.

keyPoints.heading: Extraction of metals — how reactivity determines the method
keyPoints.content: How a metal is extracted from its ore depends on its position in the reactivity series. Very reactive metals (potassium, sodium, calcium, magnesium, aluminium) cannot be reduced by carbon — they are extracted by electrolysis of their molten compounds. This requires a lot of energy and is expensive. Moderately reactive metals (zinc, iron, nickel, tin, lead) can be extracted by reduction with carbon (coke) in a blast furnace or similar. Carbon is higher in the reactivity series than these metals (at high temperatures), so it displaces them. Unreactive metals (copper, silver, gold, platinum) are found as native metals — already uncombined in nature — or require only simple heating.
writeBullets: Very reactive (K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al) — extracted by electrolysis (can't use carbon reduction)
Moderately reactive (Zn, Fe, Ni, Sn, Pb) — extracted by reduction with carbon/coke
Unreactive (Cu, Ag, Au, Pt) — found native or extracted by simple heating/chemical methods
Electrolysis — uses electricity to break down ionic compounds; expensive
Carbon reduction — carbon acts as the reducing agent; only works if carbon is more reactive than the metal
Native metal — found uncombined in Earth's crust (e.g. gold, platinum)

keyTerms.def: Using electricity to break down an ionic compound into its elements — used for very reactive metals
Using carbon (coke) to remove oxygen from a metal oxide — works for metals less reactive than carbon
A metal found uncombined in the Earth's crust because it does not readily form compounds

keyPoints.memTip: Top of series = electrolysis (think: electric cost matches high reactivity). Middle = carbon reduction. Bottom = found free. Cover and write which method is used for aluminium, iron and gold.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Aluminium is a common exam trick — students expect carbon reduction because it's so widely used in industry, but aluminium is above carbon so it needs electrolysis. Always check the position in the series first.

0.question: Which of the following correctly lists metals in order from most to least reactive?
0.correctFeedback: Correct — Na > Mg > Zn > Fe > Cu matches the reactivity series order.
0.wrongFeedback: The correct order from most to least reactive is Na, Mg, Zn, Fe, Cu. Sodium is near the top of the series, copper near the bottom. Remember: the higher the metal, the more readily it loses electrons and the more vigorously it reacts.

2.question: Iron is added to copper sulfate solution. What is observed, and why?
options: No reaction, because iron is less reactive than copper
The solution turns green and copper metal forms, because iron displaces copper
Iron dissolves completely and the solution turns blue
Copper sulfate decomposes into copper oxide and sulfur dioxide
2.correctFeedback: Correct — iron is more reactive than copper, so it displaces copper from the solution. A copper coating forms on the iron and the blue colour of the solution fades.
2.wrongFeedback: Iron is higher in the reactivity series than copper, so it can displace copper from copper sulfate solution. You'd see a reddish-brown copper coating form on the iron, and the blue colour of the copper sulfate solution fades as iron sulfate (pale green) forms.

3.question: Why is aluminium extracted by electrolysis rather than reduction with carbon?
options: Because aluminium is too heavy for carbon to lift out
Because aluminium melts at too low a temperature
Because aluminium is more reactive than carbon, so carbon cannot reduce it
Because aluminium reacts violently with carbon
3.correctFeedback: Correct — aluminium sits above carbon in the reactivity series, so carbon is not reactive enough to reduce aluminium oxide. Electrolysis must be used instead.
3.wrongFeedback: The key rule is: carbon reduction only works if the metal is less reactive than carbon. Aluminium is more reactive than carbon (at standard temperatures), so carbon cannot displace it from its oxide. Electrolysis — which uses electrical energy — is needed instead.

commonMistakes: Thinking aluminium is extracted by carbon reduction — it is above carbon in the reactivity series, so electrolysis is needed.
Confusing displacement direction — only a MORE reactive metal can displace a LESS reactive one. Copper cannot displace iron.
Forgetting that sodium/potassium react with water to give a hydroxide, not an oxide. The product is sodium hydroxide + hydrogen.
Writing the wrong salt name — hydrochloric acid gives chlorides, sulfuric acid gives sulfates, nitric acid gives nitrates.
Assuming that all metals react with cold water — only K, Na, Li, Ca react noticeably. Mg, Zn, Fe do not react visibly with cold water.
examTips: In displacement reaction questions, always identify which metal is higher in the reactivity series — that metal will displace the other.
When asked about electrolysis vs carbon reduction, the deciding factor is whether the metal is above or below carbon in the reactivity series.
Always include the state symbols (s), (l), (g), (aq) when writing full symbol equations if the question asks for them.
For ionic equations in displacement reactions, cancel the spectator ions and show only the species that change: e.g. Fe + Cu²⁺ → Fe²⁺ + Cu.
revisionCardBullets: Reactivity series order (top to bottom): K, Na, Li, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Ni, Sn, Pb, Cu, Ag, Au, Pt
More reactive = loses electrons more easily = reacts more vigorously
K, Na, Li react violently with cold water → metal hydroxide + hydrogen
Mg, Zn, Fe react with dilute acid → salt + hydrogen (no reaction with cold water for Zn/Fe)
Cu, Ag, Au — no reaction with water or dilute acid
Displacement: more reactive metal displaces less reactive from its compound
OIL RIG — Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)
Ionic equation for iron + copper sulfate: Fe + Cu²⁺ → Fe²⁺ + Cu
Very reactive metals (K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al) — extracted by electrolysis
Moderately reactive metals (Zn, Fe) — extracted by reduction with carbon
Unreactive metals (Cu, Ag, Au) — found native or simple extraction
Aluminium is above carbon — must use electrolysis, NOT carbon reduction
